common_patterns.overview:
Reusable question patterns, interview structures, and deliverable generation systems. The appropriate deliverable system is selected based on project_type from vision-config.json.
PROJECT TYPE → DELIVERABLE SYSTEM: - gui: Interactive Mock HTML Generation (screens/*.html with wireframe aesthetic + tooltips) - cli: Terminal Session Transcript Generation (commands/*.session.md with realistic output) - api: OpenAPI Contract Generation (endpoints/*.openapi.yaml) - library: Usage Example Generation (public-api/*.example.* in target language) - game: HTML Wireframes (UI only) + Mechanics Spec Generation (mechanics/*.md) - pipeline: Mermaid Data Flow Diagram Generation (in pipeline-stages/*.md)
CRITICAL DEFAULT: Always prefer offering suggested responses (2-4 concrete options) over open-ended questions. The user picks, combines, or rejects options — this is faster and less fatiguing than "what do you want?" Only fall back to open-ended questions when the user's direction is truly novel.

suggested_responses_pattern.name: Default Questioning: Suggested Responses
suggested_responses_pattern.description: This is the PRIMARY questioning technique. Use it for every decision where standard patterns exist. Offer 2-4 concrete options with brief tradeoffs.
suggested_responses_pattern.template: "For [decision], here are the common approaches: 1. [Option A] — [1-line tradeoff/benefit] 2. [Option B] — [1-line tradeoff/benefit] 3. [Option C] — [1-line tradeoff/benefit] Which fits your vision? Or do you have something different in mind?"

rules.rule: Always provide at least 2 options. Never say "what do you want?" without options to react to.
Options should be meaningfully different, not variations of the same idea.
Include tradeoffs (not just descriptions) so the user understands implications.
If the user picks an option, drill into specifics using the detailed question patterns below.
If the user rejects all options, switch to open-ended mode — they have a genuinely novel vision.

pattern.name: screen_decomposition
pattern.description: For describing any UI screen in exhaustive detail. Use during GUI/game Phase 2 screen interviews.

questions.question: What is the purpose of this screen? What does the user accomplish here?
What is the layout? (single panel, split panel, tabs, cards, list+detail, etc.)
What are the major UI regions? (header, sidebar, main content, footer, etc.)
For each region: what elements does it contain? Buttons, inputs, tables, charts, etc.?
What data is displayed in each element? Where does it come from conceptually?

What actions can the user take from this screen? Navigation, CRUD, export, etc.? FOR EACH ACTION: "What exactly happens when the user does this?" Capture the full behavioral description — this will become the tooltip text in the interactive mock.
What is the empty state? (first visit, no data, nothing selected)
What is the loading state? (spinners, skeletons, progress bars)
What is the error state? (failed to load, permission denied, network error)

How does the user get to this screen? From where? How do they leave? FOR EACH NAVIGATION TARGET: "Which screen does this go to?" If it's a new screen not yet designed, add it to state.json planned.screens.

pattern.name: command_decomposition
pattern.description: For describing a CLI command/subcommand in exhaustive detail.

questions.question: What is the purpose of this command? What does the user accomplish by running it?
What are the subcommands? (if this is a command group)
What positional arguments does it accept? Name, type, required/optional, description for each.
What flags/options does it accept? Short form (-f), long form (--flag), type, default value, required?
What input sources does it use? (stdin, file path, environment variable, config file)
What output formats does it support? (table, JSON, YAML, plain text, custom --output flag)
What are all exit codes? (0=success, specific non-zero codes for each error condition)
What error conditions exist? Invalid input, missing required args, permission denied, network failure?
How does this command interact with others? (piping output to another command, chaining, scripting)
What would a realistic terminal session look like? Show me the commands a user would type and the output.

pattern.name: endpoint_decomposition
pattern.description: For describing an API endpoint/resource group in exhaustive detail.

questions.question: What resource does this endpoint group manage? What can clients do with it?
What HTTP methods are supported? (GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, DELETE) For each: path, summary, idempotency.
What request parameters exist? (path params, query params, headers, request body with full schema)
What authentication is required? (none, API key, JWT, OAuth2 scopes) Per-endpoint or global?
What are the success response schemas? (200, 201, 204 — full JSON structure, types, required fields)
What are the error responses? (400 validation, 401 unauthorized, 403 forbidden, 404 not found, 409 conflict, 429 rate limit, 500 server error)
What pagination, filtering, and sorting options exist?
What rate limits apply? Per-endpoint or global? What headers communicate limits?
How do endpoints relate? (e.g., POST /projects/{id}/tasks creates a task under a project)
What webhooks or events does this resource trigger?

pattern.name: public_api_decomposition
pattern.description: For describing a library module/namespace's public API surface.

questions.question: What is the purpose of this module? What problem does it solve for the developer?
What are the public exports? (functions, classes, types, constants, decorators)
For each function: full signature (parameters with types, return type, async/sync, generics)
For each parameter: name, type, required/optional, default value, valid range/constraints
What errors/exceptions does each function throw? Under what conditions?
What are the side effects? (I/O, mutation, network calls, state changes)
What is the thread safety model? Is it safe for concurrent use?
How does this module compose with other modules? What are typical usage patterns?
What would realistic usage code look like? Show me how a developer would use these exports.

pattern.name: mechanics_decomposition
pattern.description: For describing a game mechanic/system in exhaustive detail.

questions.question: What is this mechanic? What player experience does it create?
What is the core loop/cycle? (trigger → player action → system response → feedback → repeat)
What are the rules and constraints? (what can/cannot happen, limits, cooldowns)
What player inputs drive this mechanic? (button presses, mouse movement, combos, timing windows)
For each input: what is the exact behavior? Frame data, animation triggers, state transitions.
What are the balance parameters? (speeds, damage values, durations, probabilities — specific numbers)
What are the edge cases? (simultaneous inputs, boundary conditions, interaction with other mechanics)
What feedback does the player receive? (visual effects, sound, camera shake, UI updates)
How does this mechanic interact with other systems? (combat + movement, inventory + equipment, etc.)
What is the failure state? What happens when the player fails at this mechanic?

pattern.name: pipeline_stage_decomposition
pattern.description: For describing a data pipeline stage in exhaustive detail.

questions.question: What is the purpose of this stage? What transformation does it perform on the data?
What is the input? (source system, data format/schema, volume, frequency/batch size)
What are the transformation steps? Each step: logic, validations, enrichments, filters.
What is the output? (destination, format/schema, write mode — append/overwrite/upsert)
What error handling exists? (malformed data → dead letter queue? transient failures → retry?)
What is the retry strategy? (max retries, backoff, idempotency guarantees)
What monitoring exists? (metrics emitted, alert thresholds, log levels, tracing)
What are the dependencies? (upstream stages, external services, configuration)
How does data flow through this stage visually? What would a diagram look like?

pattern.name: feature_specification
pattern.description: For describing any feature completely. Use during Phase 1 feature-inventory interview. Universal — all project types.

questions.question: What is the user trying to accomplish with this feature?
What triggers this feature? (for GUI: button click, navigation. For CLI: command, flag. For API: HTTP request. For library: function call. For game: player action. For pipeline: schedule or event.)
Step by step, what happens from trigger to completion?
What data goes in? What data comes out?
What validations or constraints exist?
What happens when it succeeds? What does the user see?
What happens when it fails? What error messages? Recovery path?
What permissions or roles can access this feature?
Does this feature interact with other features? How?

pattern.name: user_flow
pattern.description: For describing an end-to-end user journey. Use during Phase 3 flow/workflow interviews. Universal — adapt step references by project_type.

questions.question: Who is the user? (persona, role, context)
What is their goal? What are they trying to achieve?
Where do they start? (GUI: which screen? CLI: which command? API: which endpoint? Library: which import? Game: which screen/state? Pipeline: which source?)
What steps do they take? Reference specific interface surface artifacts.
What decisions or branching points exist? (if X then Y, else Z)
What is the happy path from start to goal?
What are the alternative paths? (different choices, edge cases)
What can go wrong at each step? How is it handled?
Where does the user end up? What do they see upon completion?

pattern.name: data_concept
pattern.description: For describing a conceptual data entity (not database schema — what data exists). Use during Phase 1 data/ interviews. Universal — all project types.

questions.question: What is this thing called? (e.g., "Task," "Project," "Invoice")
What information describes it? List every attribute. (e.g., name, status, due date, assignee)
For each attribute: is it required or optional? What format? Any constraints?
How is it created? By whom? From where?
How is it updated? By whom? From where?
How is it deleted? Soft delete or hard delete? Consequences?
How does it relate to other data concepts? (belongs to, has many, references)
Where does it appear in the interface surface? (GUI: which screens? CLI: which commands? API: which endpoints? Library: which function params? Game: which UI/mechanics? Pipeline: which stage?)

consistency_callout.description: When the user describes something that matches a pattern already established, call it out to maintain consistency.
consistency_callout.template: "This sounds similar to [previously documented pattern] in [file]. Should this follow the same pattern, or is it intentionally different?"

interactive_mock_system.name: Interactive Mock HTML Generation (GUI / Game UI)
interactive_mock_system.description:
For every UI screen, generate BOTH a canonical .md spec AND an interactive .html mock. The HTML uses inline CSS and a deliberate wireframe aesthetic. The user opens the HTML in a browser to see the layout, click around, read tooltips, and navigate between screens.
CRITICAL: The HTML is assembled from pre-built components. VH writes semantic HTML with class names. The CSS is the canonical library below — copy it verbatim into every screen HTML file. Do NOT write raw CSS. Do NOT adjust the component library per screen. If a layout pattern isn't covered, use the closest available pattern.
The HTML opens via file:// — no server, no build step, no external dependencies.

css_guidance.description:
Screen HTML files must include inline CSS following the wireframe aesthetic rules defined in 2_best_practices.xml. Generate clean, consistent CSS for each screen using the layout conventions below. The CSS should cover: reset/base, wireframe banner, layout systems (sidebar, topbar, split, list-detail), card grid, tables, forms, buttons, badges, empty states, loading skeletons, modal, tabs, tooltip system, section headers, and utility classes.
Key aesthetic constraints (from 2_best_practices.xml): - Dashed borders (2px dashed) on all structural elements - Grayscale palette (#333, #666, #999, #bbb, #ddd, #eee, #f5f5f5, #f0f0f0, #fff) - Single accent color: #2563eb (blue) for primary actions and active states - Tooltip text: #ffd700 (gold) on #333 (dark) background - System-ui sans-serif font, no external fonts - Wireframe banner as first body element on every page - No shadows on cards, no animations (except skeleton loading)

tooltip_javascript.description: Minimal JavaScript for click-activated tooltips on non-navigation buttons. Navigation links use standard — no JS needed. Hover tooltips use CSS-only (.tooltip-trigger:hover). Click tooltips show a toast at the bottom of the screen. Copy this script into every screen HTML just before .
tooltip_javascript.code:
<script>
  // Click-activated tooltips for non-navigation interactive elements
  document.addEventListener('DOMContentLoaded', function() {
    var toast = document.getElementById('tooltip-toast');
    if (!toast) {
      toast = document.createElement('div');
      toast.id = 'tooltip-toast';
      toast.className = 'tooltip-toast';
      document.body.appendChild(toast);
    }

    // All elements with data-tooltip attribute that are NOT links
    var triggers = document.querySelectorAll('[data-tooltip]:not(a)');
    triggers.forEach(function(el) {
      el.addEventListener('click', function(e) {
        e.preventDefault();
        e.stopPropagation();
        var text = this.getAttribute('data-tooltip');
        toast.textContent = text;
        toast.style.display = 'block';
        clearTimeout(toast._timeout);
        toast._timeout = setTimeout(function() {
          toast.style.display = 'none';
        }, 4000);
      });
    });

    // Hide toast on click anywhere else
    document.addEventListener('click', function() {
      toast.style.display = 'none';
    });
  });
</script>

screen_html_template.description: The canonical HTML structure for every screen mock. Copy this template and fill in the body content using the CSS component library classes. The
screen_html_template.code: ⚠️ VISION WIREFRAME — NOT FINAL DESIGN — Interactive mock for product discovery only

placeholder_page_template.description: When a screen is referenced (as a navigation target) but hasn't been interviewed yet, generate a placeholder page immediately. This ensures navigation links never 404. The placeholder signals "this screen exists in the plan but hasn't been designed."
placeholder_page_template.code:
📋

[SCREEN NAME]

This screen has been referenced in the vision but hasn't been interviewed yet. It will be designed in an upcoming session.

← Go back

layout_conventions.description: How to structure screen content using the CSS component library classes:

convention.name: sidebar_layout
convention.usage: Most common layout. Sidebar navigation + main content area.
convention.code:
Navigation
Dashboard Projects ...

convention.name: topbar_layout
convention.usage: Top navigation bar + content below. For apps with global search/actions.
convention.code: [App Name] Settings

convention.name: list_detail_layout
convention.usage: Master-detail: list on left, detail on right.

convention.name: card_grid
convention.code:
[Title]

[Description]

convention.name: modal
convention.usage: Overlay dialog. Show/hide with inline style display:none/block.
convention.code: [Modal Title]

wireframe_aesthetic_rules.rule: Every screen HTML MUST include the wireframe-banner div as the first element inside .
All borders use dashed style (2px dashed #bbb / #999 / #333 depending on element).
Color palette: grayscale (#333, #666, #999, #bbb, #ddd, #eee, #f5f5f5, #f0f0f0, #fff) + one accent (#2563eb blue).
Tooltip text color is gold (#ffd700) on dark background (#333) — visually distinct from content.
Font is system-ui sans-serif. No custom fonts. No Google Fonts. No CDN links.
All interactive elements (buttons, inputs, links) must be clearly distinguishable.
Placeholder data should be realistic: "Website Redesign" not "Item 1", "test@example.com" not "xxx".
Modal overlays should be position:fixed to appear above page content.
No animations except the skeleton loading shimmer (CSS-only).
No shadows on cards or elements (except tooltip bubble and modal overlay).

tooltip_content_rules.description: Every interactive element must have a behavioral description via tooltip.
tooltip_content_rules.rule: Navigation links (): NO tooltip needed — the link itself communicates the action. But if the navigation has side effects, add a hover tooltip.
Action buttons (): MUST have data-tooltip attribute describing what WOULD happen. Format: "Would [action description]. [Result/next state]."
Form submit buttons: data-tooltip describes validation rules and success/failure behavior. Format: "Would validate: [rules]. On success: [result]. On failure: [error handling]."
Tooltip text should be 1-3 sentences. Concise but complete.
Tooltip text must match the behavioral description in the .md spec.
Hover tooltips (via .tooltip-trigger > .tooltip-bubble) for elements where the behavior needs explanation before clicking.
Click tooltips (via data-tooltip attribute + JS) for buttons where the action is the primary interaction.

mock_generation_workflow.step: 1. Interview the screen using screen_decomposition pattern.
2. Draft the .md spec first — this is the canonical specification.
3. Generate the .html mock with inline CSS following the wireframe aesthetic rules in 2_best_practices.xml. Use semantic class names for the layout conventions below.
4. For every action described in the .md spec, add the corresponding data-tooltip to the HTML element.
5. For every navigation target, add an link. If target screen doesn't exist yet, generate a placeholder.
6. Include the tooltip JavaScript just before (verbatim from the template above).
7. Present to user: "Open screens/[name].html in your browser."
8. On user feedback: regenerate the FULL HTML (do not patch individual elements).
9. On user confirmation: mark confirmed in state.json, generate placeholders for new navigation targets discovered.

terminal_transcript_system.name: Terminal Session Transcript Generation (CLI)
terminal_transcript_system.description: For every CLI command/subcommand, generate BOTH a canonical .md spec AND a terminal session transcript (.session.md). The session transcript is the CLI equivalent of a GUI interactive mock — the user reads realistic terminal output to verify behavior.

session_transcript_format.rule: Use `$ ` as the prompt prefix for all commands.
Show realistic output immediately after each command — formatted as it would appear in a real terminal (tables with borders, JSON with indentation, etc.).
Include at minimum: happy path example, --help output, one error case.
For each flag/option, show at least one example invocation.
Show pipe and redirect examples when applicable.
Exit code should be implied by success/failure — if the output shows an error message, the reader understands this would be a non-zero exit.

transcript_template.description: Template for a command session transcript file:
transcript_template.code:
# [Command Name] — Terminal Session Transcript
> These transcripts simulate realistic terminal sessions. They are the CLI equivalent of GUI wireframe mocks. > Read through them to verify: subcommands, flags, output formats, and error handling match your vision.
## Happy Path
$ mycli [command] [args] --flags [expected output — table, JSON, or plain text]
$ mycli [command] [args] --output json { "status": "success", "data": { ... } }
## Help Output
$ mycli [command] --help Usage: mycli [command] [options]
[help text showing all subcommands, flags, and arguments]
## Error Cases
$ mycli [command] --invalid-flag Error: unknown flag '--invalid-flag'. See 'mycli [command] --help'.
$ mycli [command] [missing-required-arg] Error: required argument [name] is missing. Usage: mycli [command] <required-arg> [options]

openapi_contract_system.name: OpenAPI Contract Generation (API)
openapi_contract_system.description: For every API endpoint resource group, generate BOTH a canonical .md spec AND an OpenAPI 3.0 YAML contract. The YAML can be opened in Swagger UI for interactive API reference.

openapi_standards.rule: Define reusable schemas under components/schemas and reference via $ref.
Document all error response codes with example response bodies.
Include security scheme definitions (bearerAuth, apiKey, etc.).
Tag endpoints by resource group for Swagger UI grouping.
Include request/response examples, not just schemas.

openapi_template.code: openapi: 3.0.3 info: title: [API Name] version: 1.0.0 description: [Vision spec — not final implementation] servers: - url: https://api.example.com/v1 description: Production (envisioned) security: - bearerAuth: [] paths: /[resource]: get: summary: [List resources] operationId: list[Resources] parameters: - name: page in: query schema: { type: integer, default: 1 } - name: limit in: query schema: { type: integer, default: 20, maximum: 100 } responses: '200': description: Successful response content: application/json: schema: type: object properties: data: type: array items: $ref: '#/components/schemas/[Resource]' '401': $ref: '#/components/responses/Unauthorized' components: securitySchemes: bearerAuth: type: http scheme: bearer bearerFormat: JWT schemas: [Resource]: type: object required: [id, name] properties: id: { type: string, format: uuid } name: { type: string } responses: Unauthorized: description: Authentication required content: application/json: schema: type: object properties: error: { type: string, example: "Authentication required" }

usage_example_system.name: Usage Example Generation (Library/SDK)
usage_example_system.description: For every library module, generate runnable usage example files in the target language. These are the library equivalent of GUI interactive mocks.

example_standards.rule: Examples must be in the target language (determined from vision-config.json or app-overview.md platform section).
Examples should be realistic, not contrived — show patterns a real developer would use.
Include error handling in examples (try/catch, Result types, etc.).
Add inline comments explaining the "why" for each section.
Show composition: how this module is used with other modules.

mermaid_diagram_system.name: Mermaid Data Flow Diagram Generation (Pipeline)
mermaid_diagram_system.description: For every pipeline stage, include a Mermaid graph or flowchart diagram in the .md spec showing data flow. The diagram is the pipeline equivalent of a GUI interactive mock.

mermaid_standards.rule: Use ```mermaid code blocks within the .md spec file.
Show input sources, transformation steps, output destinations, and error paths.
Use graph TD (top-down) or flowchart LR (left-right) orientation.
Label edges with data format/volume where relevant.
Show DLQ (dead letter queue) and retry paths.

mermaid_template.description: Example Mermaid diagram for a pipeline stage:
mermaid_template.code: ```mermaid graph TD A[S3 Bucket: raw-events/] -->|JSON, 10K events/min| B[Stage: Validate] B -->|Valid events| C[Stage: Transform] B -->|Invalid events| D[DLQ: invalid-events/] C -->|Enriched events| E[Stage: Load] E -->|Upsert| F[(PostgreSQL: events table)] E -->|Failure after 3 retries| D ```
